questions.scenario: Karwood Securities is a Toronto Investment Dealer with a twelve-person research department. Analyst Ingrid Solberg covers Drayford Composites, an industrial materials issuer, and her new report is in final review with designated research Supervisor Mateo Cruz. The file shows: (1) Karwood is currently engaged, for a fee, as financial adviser to Drayford on its proposed acquisition of a U.S. competitor, an engagement that began four months ago; (2) at the end of last month — the report will be issued on the 25th of this month — Karwood and its affiliates held 1.8% of Drayford's common shares; (3) the report appears in a six-page single-issuer format with a 'Buy' recommendation. Meanwhile, an institutional sales trader on Karwood's desk learns through office chatter that Ingrid is about to initiate coverage on a second issuer, Bessamer Tools, with a favourable first-time recommendation, and quietly buys Bessamer shares for his personal account two days before that initiation is issued. Separately, Mateo Cruz himself has authored a report on a bank he personally covers, and the head of research asks who can approve it.
questions.stem: The sales trader argues his Bessamer purchase is untouchable by Karwood's research trading policies because the upcoming publication is a first-time initiation, 'not a change to anything.' How should the firm's IDPC 3619(1) policies treat his trade?

options.A: As permitted — 3619(1) covers only changes to existing recommendations
options.B: As permitted, because sales traders are exempt from research trading policies
options.C: As trading the policies must detect and restrict — 3619(1) covers trading done with knowledge of or in anticipation of the issuance of a research report, a new recommendation, or a change in a recommendation that could reasonably be expected to affect the price
options.D: As permitted, provided he discloses the trade in next quarter's attestation

questions.rationale: Subsection 3619(1) requires policies and procedures that specifically address detecting and restricting any trading in equity securities or equity related securities of a subject issuer done with knowledge of or in anticipation of (i) the issuance of a research report, (ii) a new recommendation, or (iii) a change in a recommendation, where it could reasonably be expected to affect the price — a first-time favourable initiation is both the issuance of a research report and 'a new recommendation,' so buying ahead of it on office chatter is precisely what the policies must detect and restrict. Option A is the closest distractor: it isolates clause (iii) and ignores clauses (i) and (ii), which exist to close exactly this initiation gap. Option B has no basis — the subsection addresses any such trading, not particular job categories — and option D substitutes after-the-fact attestation for detection and restriction.
questions.source_quote: "(ii) a new recommendation, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6034

questions.scenario: Karwood Securities is a Toronto Investment Dealer with a twelve-person research department. Analyst Ingrid Solberg covers Drayford Composites, an industrial materials issuer, and her new report is in final review with designated research Supervisor Mateo Cruz. The file shows: (1) Karwood is currently engaged, for a fee, as financial adviser to Drayford on its proposed acquisition of a U.S. competitor, an engagement that began four months ago; (2) at the end of last month — the report will be issued on the 25th of this month — Karwood and its affiliates held 1.8% of Drayford's common shares; (3) the report appears in a six-page single-issuer format with a 'Buy' recommendation. Meanwhile, an institutional sales trader on Karwood's desk learns through office chatter that Ingrid is about to initiate coverage on a second issuer, Bessamer Tools, with a favourable first-time recommendation, and quietly buys Bessamer shares for his personal account two days before that initiation is issued. Separately, Mateo Cruz himself has authored a report on a bank he personally covers, and the head of research asks who can approve it.
questions.stem: Mateo Cruz, the designated research Supervisor, has himself authored a report on a bank he covers. He proposes signing his own approval. Using IDPC 3607(2) and the syllabus 8.4 parameters, what should the head of research require instead?

options.A: Self-approval, since Mateo is the most qualified reviewer
options.B: Review and approval by another designated Supervisor — the rule permits designating one or more Supervisors to be responsible for reviewing and approving research reports, and the syllabus identifies cross-supervision as the answer when the designated Supervisor's own work needs approval
options.C: Approval by the head of investment banking as a neutral senior party
options.D: Publication without approval, since Supervisors are exempt from review

questions.rationale: Subsection 3607(2) requires the dealer to designate one or more Supervisors to be responsible for reviewing and approving research reports, and the syllabus 8.4 parameter pairs that designation requirement with 'Cross-supervision' — the arrangement under which a second designated Supervisor reviews and approves material produced by the first, preserving an independent check when the designated Supervisor is the author. Option A is the closest distractor: expertise does not solve the self-review problem the approval requirement exists to prevent. Option C is independently prohibited — clause 3621(2)(i) requires policies prohibiting the approval of research reports by the investment banking department — and option D abandons the 3602(3)(i) requirement that research reports be approved before use or publication.
questions.source_quote:
"Designating one or more Supervisors to be responsible for reviewing
and approving research reports
- Cross-supervision" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 1544-1546

questions.scenario: Ossington Bluffs Securities runs a spring marketing campaign out of its Winnipeg head office. Designated advertising Supervisor Sylvie Kowalczyk audits the campaign and finds: (i) Registered Representative Joaquin Stannard has added the designation 'Family Stewardship Counsellor' to his client emails — the firm formally approved this designation last year, but only for his colleague Greta Iverson, the person who completed the issuing program; (ii) an email blast was sent to 4,000 purchased addresses of people with no relationship to the firm, with no consent records, though its investment content satisfied every CIRO content standard; (iii) a live 'Spring Portfolio Strategies' webinar deck pitching the firm's new managed program is scheduled for next Tuesday, and the deck has not yet been approved by anyone; and (iv) the campaign's centrepiece print advertisement was created by an outside advertising agency, whose creative director 'signed off on compliance' before it ran in two newspapers, with no internal review of any kind before publication.
questions.stem: Item (i): the firm approved the 'Family Stewardship Counsellor' designation for Greta Iverson. May Joaquin Stannard use it in his client emails under IDPC 3640(2)?

options.A: Yes — once a dealer approves a designation, every Approved Person at the firm may use it
options.B: Yes, provided Joaquin enrols in the issuing program within a year
options.C: No — clause 3640(2)(iii) prohibits use where the dealer has not approved the use by that Approved Person of the title or designation, and Ossington Bluffs approved its use by Greta Iverson, not by Joaquin
options.D: No, because designations may never appear in client emails

questions.rationale: Clause 3640(2)(iii) prohibits a client-facing Approved Person from using a title or designation 'if the Approved Person's Dealer Member has not approved the use by that Approved Person' of it — the approval is person-specific, so the firm's approval of Greta Iverson's use does not authorize Joaquin's, and his emails breach the clause until the firm approves his own use. Option A is the closest distractor: it converts a per-person approval into a firm-wide licence, contradicting the clause's 'by that Approved Person' wording. Option B invents a grace-period cure, and option D overshoots — approved designations may be used; the rule controls who has approval, not the email medium.
questions.source_quote: "if the Approved Person’s Dealer Member has not approved the use by that Approved Person" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6113

questions.scenario: Ossington Bluffs Securities runs a spring marketing campaign out of its Winnipeg head office. Designated advertising Supervisor Sylvie Kowalczyk audits the campaign and finds: (i) Registered Representative Joaquin Stannard has added the designation 'Family Stewardship Counsellor' to his client emails — the firm formally approved this designation last year, but only for his colleague Greta Iverson, the person who completed the issuing program; (ii) an email blast was sent to 4,000 purchased addresses of people with no relationship to the firm, with no consent records, though its investment content satisfied every CIRO content standard; (iii) a live 'Spring Portfolio Strategies' webinar deck pitching the firm's new managed program is scheduled for next Tuesday, and the deck has not yet been approved by anyone; and (iv) the campaign's centrepiece print advertisement was created by an outside advertising agency, whose creative director 'signed off on compliance' before it ran in two newspapers, with no internal review of any kind before publication.
questions.stem: Item (ii): the email blast met every CIRO content standard but went to purchased addresses with no consent records. Why can Sylvie still treat the blast as a breach of IDPC 3602(1)?

options.A: Because purchased mailing lists are banned by name in Rule 3600
options.B: Because all marketing emails require CIRO pre-clearance
options.C: Because emails may never be sent to more than 1,000 recipients
options.D: Because clause 3602(1)(vii) prohibits communications that fail to comply with Corporation requirements or any applicable laws — and the syllabus identifies Canadian Anti-spam Legislation among the legislative requirements relevant to supervising client communications, so satisfying CIRO content standards alone does not make the blast compliant

questions.rationale: Clause 3602(1)(vii) prohibits any advertisement, sales literature or correspondence that fails to comply with Corporation requirements or any applicable laws — the clause incorporates external law into the communications standard, and the syllabus 8.1 parameter expressly lists 'Legislative requirements, including Canadian Anti-spam Legislation,' the regime governing consent for commercial electronic messages. A blast to purchased addresses without consent records can therefore breach clause (vii) even though every CIRO content rule was met. Option A is the closest distractor: Rule 3600 names no mailing-list ban; the defect arrives through the applicable-laws clause. Options B and C invent a CIRO pre-clearance regime and a recipient cap that exist nowhere in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "fails to comply with Corporation requirements or any applicable laws." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5863

questions.scenario: Ossington Bluffs Securities runs a spring marketing campaign out of its Winnipeg head office. Designated advertising Supervisor Sylvie Kowalczyk audits the campaign and finds: (i) Registered Representative Joaquin Stannard has added the designation 'Family Stewardship Counsellor' to his client emails — the firm formally approved this designation last year, but only for his colleague Greta Iverson, the person who completed the issuing program; (ii) an email blast was sent to 4,000 purchased addresses of people with no relationship to the firm, with no consent records, though its investment content satisfied every CIRO content standard; (iii) a live 'Spring Portfolio Strategies' webinar deck pitching the firm's new managed program is scheduled for next Tuesday, and the deck has not yet been approved by anyone; and (iv) the campaign's centrepiece print advertisement was created by an outside advertising agency, whose creative director 'signed off on compliance' before it ran in two newspapers, with no internal review of any kind before publication.
questions.stem: Item (iii): the 'Spring Portfolio Strategies' webinar pitching the firm's new managed program is set for Tuesday, and its deck is unapproved. The presenter argues a live webinar 'isn't a seminar' under the rule. What must Sylvie require?

options.A: The deck must be approved by a designated Supervisor before the webinar — it is a promotional seminar text within 3602(3)(iv), and presenting it in a virtual format does not move it off the pre-approval list
options.B: Nothing — webinars are spoken events outside Rule 3600 entirely
options.C: Recording the webinar and reviewing the recording within a week
options.D: Approval is needed only if attendees pay an admission fee

questions.rationale: Clause 3602(3)(iv) places promotional seminar texts (excluding educational seminar texts) on the list of items the dealer must ensure are approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication — a deck built to pitch the firm's new managed program is promotional seminar text, and nothing in the clause confines 'seminar' to in-person formats, so the Tuesday session cannot proceed on an unapproved deck. Option C is the closest distractor: record-and-review is a 3602(4) post-use method, available only for material not listed in 3602(3). Option B wrongly exempts a prepared, presented text because the event is live, and option D invents an admission-fee trigger; what matters is the promotional character of the text.
questions.source_quote: "(iv)     promotional seminar texts (excluding educational seminar texts)," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5871

questions.scenario: Ossington Bluffs Securities runs a spring marketing campaign out of its Winnipeg head office. Designated advertising Supervisor Sylvie Kowalczyk audits the campaign and finds: (i) Registered Representative Joaquin Stannard has added the designation 'Family Stewardship Counsellor' to his client emails — the firm formally approved this designation last year, but only for his colleague Greta Iverson, the person who completed the issuing program; (ii) an email blast was sent to 4,000 purchased addresses of people with no relationship to the firm, with no consent records, though its investment content satisfied every CIRO content standard; (iii) a live 'Spring Portfolio Strategies' webinar deck pitching the firm's new managed program is scheduled for next Tuesday, and the deck has not yet been approved by anyone; and (iv) the campaign's centrepiece print advertisement was created by an outside advertising agency, whose creative director 'signed off on compliance' before it ran in two newspapers, with no internal review of any kind before publication.
questions.stem: Item (iv): the centrepiece print advertisement ran after the outside agency's creative director 'signed off on compliance,' with no internal review before publication. Applying IDPC 3602(3), why does the agency sign-off fail?

options.A: Because outside agencies may design but never write advertising copy
options.B: Because newspaper advertising requires CIRO approval rather than agency approval
options.C: Because agency sign-offs are valid only when countersigned by the firm's lawyers
options.D: Because the rule places the obligation on the Dealer Member to ensure that original advertisements are approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication — outsourcing production does not transfer the approval function to the vendor

questions.rationale: Subsection 3602(3) opens with 'A Dealer Member must ensure that the following items are approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication,' and original advertisements are on the list — the approval must come from a designated Supervisor of the dealer, and the dealer's duty to ensure it cannot be discharged by an external agency's creative director, however confident the sign-off. Option C is the closest distractor: it at least recognizes the agency sign-off is insufficient, but it substitutes legal countersignature for the designated-Supervisor approval the rule names. Option A invents a design-versus-copy restriction, and option B invents CIRO approval of dealer advertising, which the rule does not require.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must ensure that the following items are approved by a designated Supervisor" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5866

questions.scenario: Vanstone Capital Markets is an Investment Dealer with an active equity research group. Within one week, four matters land on the desk of designated research Supervisor Adaeze Nwabueze: (1) Vanstone co-managed an offering of Trembleau Energy's equity securities, and the syndicate desk advises that the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under the market-stabilization provisions in Corporation requirements and securities laws, while the research team asks whether the usual post-offering publication blackout applies; (2) a compliance audit reports that conversations between Vanstone analysts and investment banking staff about issuers under current research coverage occur freely by phone and hallway, with no system anywhere that controls or records that information flow; (3) analyst Bruno Akhtar discloses that he has just accepted an unpaid seat on the volunteer board of a community housing charity and argues no firm sign-off is needed since no securities and no pay are involved; and (4) analyst Mei-Lin Cho, with the firm's written pre-approval, has for six months provided paid strategic advice to Quarstadt Minerals — and now proposes to initiate research coverage on Quarstadt herself.
questions.stem: Matter (1): the subject securities of the Trembleau Energy offering are exempted under the market-stabilization provisions. Does the IDPC 3622(1) quiet period bind Vanstone's research on Trembleau?

options.A: Yes — stabilization status is irrelevant to research timing
options.B: Yes, but the period shrinks from 10 days to five
options.C: No — subsection 3622(3) provides that 3622(1) does not apply where the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under provisions relating to market stabilization set out in Corporation requirements and securities laws
options.D: No, because co-managers were never subject to quiet periods

questions.rationale: Subsection 3622(3) states that subsection 3622(1) does not apply where the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under provisions relating to market stabilization set out in Corporation requirements and securities laws — on the syndicate desk's advice that the exemption applies, the quiet period in 3622(1) does not bind the research. Option A is the closest distractor: it asserts the general co-manager prohibition while missing the express carve-out in 3622(3). Option B invents a shortened period found nowhere in the rule, and option D is wrong on the law — 3622(1) expressly applies to a dealer that has acted as manager or co-manager, so absent the exemption the quiet period would bind Vanstone.
questions.source_quote: "under provisions relating to market stabilization set out in Corporation requirements and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6082

questions.scenario: Vanstone Capital Markets is an Investment Dealer with an active equity research group. Within one week, four matters land on the desk of designated research Supervisor Adaeze Nwabueze: (1) Vanstone co-managed an offering of Trembleau Energy's equity securities, and the syndicate desk advises that the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under the market-stabilization provisions in Corporation requirements and securities laws, while the research team asks whether the usual post-offering publication blackout applies; (2) a compliance audit reports that conversations between Vanstone analysts and investment banking staff about issuers under current research coverage occur freely by phone and hallway, with no system anywhere that controls or records that information flow; (3) analyst Bruno Akhtar discloses that he has just accepted an unpaid seat on the volunteer board of a community housing charity and argues no firm sign-off is needed since no securities and no pay are involved; and (4) analyst Mei-Lin Cho, with the firm's written pre-approval, has for six months provided paid strategic advice to Quarstadt Minerals — and now proposes to initiate research coverage on Quarstadt herself.
questions.stem: Matter (2): the audit found no system anywhere that controls or records the flow of information between analysts and investment banking staff about covered issuers. Which minimum requirement of IDPC 3621(2) is Vanstone missing?

options.A: The requirement that policies and procedures address establishing systems to control and record the flow of information between analysts and investment banking department staff regarding issuers that are the subject of current or prospective research reports
options.B: The requirement that analysts and bankers work on separate floors of the building
options.C: The requirement that all analyst-banker conversations be chaperoned by external counsel
options.D: The requirement to file conversation logs with CIRO monthly

questions.rationale: Clause 3621(2)(iv) requires the dealer's policies and procedures to specifically address, at a minimum, establishing systems to control and record the flow of information between analysts and investment banking department staff, regarding issuers that are the subject of current or prospective research reports — uncontrolled, unrecorded phone and hallway exchanges about covered issuers mean the required systems simply do not exist. Option B is the closest distractor: physical separation is one way firms implement information barriers, but the clause mandates control-and-record systems, not a floor plan. Options C and D invent chaperoning by external counsel and CIRO log filings, neither of which appears in the clause.

questions.scenario: Vanstone Capital Markets is an Investment Dealer with an active equity research group. Within one week, four matters land on the desk of designated research Supervisor Adaeze Nwabueze: (1) Vanstone co-managed an offering of Trembleau Energy's equity securities, and the syndicate desk advises that the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under the market-stabilization provisions in Corporation requirements and securities laws, while the research team asks whether the usual post-offering publication blackout applies; (2) a compliance audit reports that conversations between Vanstone analysts and investment banking staff about issuers under current research coverage occur freely by phone and hallway, with no system anywhere that controls or records that information flow; (3) analyst Bruno Akhtar discloses that he has just accepted an unpaid seat on the volunteer board of a community housing charity and argues no firm sign-off is needed since no securities and no pay are involved; and (4) analyst Mei-Lin Cho, with the firm's written pre-approval, has for six months provided paid strategic advice to Quarstadt Minerals — and now proposes to initiate research coverage on Quarstadt herself.
questions.stem: Matter (3): Bruno Akhtar contends his unpaid charity board seat needs no firm sign-off because it involves no securities and no compensation. How does IDPC 3623(1) apply?

options.A: He is right — only paid outside activities require approval
options.B: He is wrong — the rule states that a Dealer Member must pre-approve an analyst's outside activities, without qualification as to payment or subject matter, so the board seat requires the firm's pre-approval
options.C: He is right — charitable roles are expressly exempted by the rule
options.D: He is wrong, but only because housing charities can issue bonds

questions.rationale: Subsection 3623(1) states in unqualified terms that a Dealer Member must pre-approve an analyst's outside activities — the text draws no line between paid and unpaid roles or between securities-related and other activities, so the charity board seat requires pre-approval before he takes it up; vetting even benign-seeming roles is how the firm detects relationships that could later trigger the 3614 issuing prohibition or 3608 disclosures, and the syllabus pairs analyst conduct with outside activities. Option A is the closest distractor: it implies a remuneration threshold the one-sentence rule does not contain. Option C invents a charitable exemption, and option D reaches the right conclusion through an irrelevant speculation about the charity's financing rather than the rule's unqualified wording.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must pre-approve an analyst’s outside activities." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6085

questions.scenario: Vanstone Capital Markets is an Investment Dealer with an active equity research group. Within one week, four matters land on the desk of designated research Supervisor Adaeze Nwabueze: (1) Vanstone co-managed an offering of Trembleau Energy's equity securities, and the syndicate desk advises that the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under the market-stabilization provisions in Corporation requirements and securities laws, while the research team asks whether the usual post-offering publication blackout applies; (2) a compliance audit reports that conversations between Vanstone analysts and investment banking staff about issuers under current research coverage occur freely by phone and hallway, with no system anywhere that controls or records that information flow; (3) analyst Bruno Akhtar discloses that he has just accepted an unpaid seat on the volunteer board of a community housing charity and argues no firm sign-off is needed since no securities and no pay are involved; and (4) analyst Mei-Lin Cho, with the firm's written pre-approval, has for six months provided paid strategic advice to Quarstadt Minerals — and now proposes to initiate research coverage on Quarstadt herself.
questions.stem: Matter (4): Mei-Lin Cho's paid strategic-advice role at Quarstadt Minerals carries the firm's written pre-approval, and she now wants to publish research initiating coverage on Quarstadt. May Vanstone issue her report?

options.A: Yes — the 3623 pre-approval immunizes any consequence of the outside activity
options.B: Yes, provided the advisory role is disclosed prominently in the report
options.C: Yes, if a second analyst co-signs the report
options.D: No — IDPC 3614 prohibits the dealer from issuing a research report prepared by an analyst on any issuer for which the analyst serves in any advisory capacity, and the 3623 pre-approval of the outside activity does not convert that issuing prohibition into a disclosure matter

questions.rationale: Section 3614 prohibits a Dealer Member from issuing a research report prepared by an analyst on any issuer for which the analyst, an associate of the analyst or the designated Supervisor serves as an officer, director or employee of the issuer, or serves in any advisory capacity to the issuer — Mei-Lin's ongoing paid strategic-advice role is service in an advisory capacity, so her Quarstadt report cannot be issued; pre-approval under 3623 governs whether she may hold the outside role, not whether the dealer may publish her research on that issuer. Option B is the closest distractor: it treats 3614 as if it were a 3608 disclosure item, but 3614 is a prohibition on issuing, which disclosure cannot satisfy. Option A overreads the effect of outside-activity approval, and option C's co-signature does not change that the report was prepared by an analyst with the prohibited relationship.
questions.source_quote: "(ii)    serves in any advisory capacity to the issuer." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5999
